narrative:
Today will be the pick day of the Easter weekend if you're wanting to spend some time outside. Temperatures will still be warm, but we'll have to dodge some pop-up showers and a couple of thundershowers, mainly this afternoon and lingering into the evening. This will give way to an overnight break, until steadier rain moves in late in the night. Easter Sunrise services look to be damp as a cold front comes through. Rain diminishes by afternoon as gusty winds and cooler temperatures move in. Cooler weather lingers through much of next week, with a hard freeze on tap for Tuesday night.

The RaysWeather.Com Photo Contest is underway for photos to appear in the 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar. We are open for submissions through April 15. We broke all records with 2026 calendar sales and look to top that with the 2027 calendar — it's our 24th calendar. Come on! Hit us with your best shot!

The "Mix and Match" Special Offer continues on all the Snowman O'Mometer Pint Glass and Mug (or you can long for a 5 Golfball Day with that pint glass as well). Buy one, and get another drinkware item at 40% off.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Friday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion Warm southerly flow ahead of an approaching cold front will push a batch of moisture into the region today, and this will increase the coverage of afternoon showers and a few thundershowers. Most spots will see at least a little rain this afternoon or evening, but there should be plenty of dry time as well. After today's activity fades away this evening, we'll have a short break tonight before rain with the front arrives during the predawn hours. The front brings fairly steady rain for several hours on Easter Sunday morning, but tapers off during the midday hours as brisk, cooler, drier air pushes in. There may be some breaks in the clouds to finish out Easter Sunday. Most of next week looks quiet, but seasonably cool, with temperatures a little below average for early April. A hard freeze looks very likely Tuesday night. All of this seasonable chill is due to a large area of high pressure way up over the Northwest Territories of Canada this weekend. The high will trek southward, crossing into the United States on Monday, then eastward across the lower Great Lakes Tuesday. The high will move out into the North Atlantic on Wednesday, but will leave a ridge extending into our area for the rest of the week. Southerly flow should see temperatures rebound by Thursday and Friday. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Apr 02, 2026 April 2026 Outlook Monthly Forecast Southern Appalachians – SW Virginia & Western North Carolina The April Pattern: Bumpy Road toward Summer Spring in the Southern Appalachians is often characterized by teasing warmth and chilly disappointments, but ultimately, we are progressing toward warmer weather. This April will fit that mold very well. March and April tend to be wetter than the winter months, but not as wet as May through August. And frost… For NW NC and SW VA, “don’t plant until Mother’s Day” is always the best advice. SW NC and the Foothills are typically safe for planting about a week or so before Mother’s Day. The NE Foothills are usually a bit earlier still. Snow? The Foothills are typically “done” by April, but the mountain region can still have an anomalous snow event. It’s worth noting that the biggest snow event ever recorded in NC occurred atop Mt. Pisgah in the first week of May 1992. Bottom Line for April 2026 Temperatures will average near normal for the month, but with numerous “ups” and “downs” in a fast westerly flow. Precipitation is expected to be near average as well, with a core of wetter weather to our west from Texas to the Ohio Valley. If we reach average rainfall, it will come from the frequency of rain events, probably not from a few heavy events. While stormy weather will occur as we move toward warmer weather, we do not expect a particularly stormy month. April Normals for Various Locations in the Southern Appalachians (Temperatures in degrees F, Precipitation and Snow in inches)
Location Avg High Avg Low Avg Precip Avg Snow
Asheville 68.9 45.8 3.69 0.3
Asheville Airport 69.4 44.6 4.17 0.2
Banner Elk 60.4 38.6 4.83 0.8
Beech Mtn 53.3 36.4 4.92 4.4
Boone 61.7 36.9 5.52 1.0
Burnsville 66.0 41.9 4.21 0.5
Galax 64.7 39.4 4.07 0.5
Hendersonville 68.3 42.8 4.84 0.0
Hickory 70.0 47.7 4.08 0.0
Jefferson 63.1 36.3 4.78 0.0
Lenoir 70.1 45.0 4.44 0.0
Marion 69.8 45.9 4.90 0.0
Marshall 68.8 38.3 3.71 0.8
Morganton 70.6 44.9 4.29 0.0
Mt. Mitchell 50.2 32.0 6.52 7.1
North Wilkesboro 69.9 43.6 4.72 0.0
Sparta 63.4 39.8 5.36 1.0
Waynesville 65.6 39.6 4.54 0.4
Wytheville 64.5 39.2 3.75 0.5
Temperatures: Near Average but Variable The overall pattern for April features a progressive flow across the United States, with a mean trough positioned to our west and later to our north—centered from the northern Plains into eastern Canada—and a ridge anchored across the southern U.S. That places the Southern Appalachians in between, in a corridor of fast-moving west-to-east flow. In this setup, systems do not stall. Instead, fronts move through every few days, bringing a warm first few days followed by alternating periods of warmth ahead of each system and cooler air behind it. The pattern does not lock into either a warm or cold regime, but keeps the weather changing regularly through the month. The following 7-day 500 mb height map illustrates a common pattern during the month. It shows a broad, relatively flat west-to-east flow across the United States, with no strong blocking in place. The Southern Appalachians sit between cooler air to the north and warmer air to the south, with systems moving steadily along this boundary. This setup favors frequent changes in air masses rather than a prolonged warm or cold stretch, which is why temperatures trend near average overall but swing up and down every few days. Precipitation: Frequent, not Prolonged Rain The progressive pattern brings precipitation in frequent periods rather than prolonged ones. Fronts move through every few days, each bringing a period of rain followed by a break. Systems don’t stall, so extended multi-day rain is unlikely, but the pattern also doesn’t shut down, so long dry stretches are unlikely. Overall, rainfall is driven by frequency rather than intensity, supporting near-normal totals. Western sections of the Appalachians may receive more rain, with totals decreasing eastward into the Foothills. April Outlook at a Glance
Week Temperature Precipitation
Week 1 (Apr 1–7) Above average but turning cooler toward the end of the period Near average
Week 2 (Apr 8–14) Near to slightly above average Near average
Week 3 (Apr 15–21) Near average Near average
Week 4 (Apr 22–30) Slightly cooler than average Near average
Overall for April Near average Near average
Factors Considered in this Forecast Several large-scale climate factors contributed to these forecast decisions: AO (Arctic Oscillation): Expected to remain near neutral, not favoring a deeper trough in the Eastern U.S. NAO (North Atlantic Oscillation): Near neutral to slightly positive, suggesting no prolonged deep trough in the East PNA (Pacific–North American): Negative, favoring a trough in the Western U.S. and ridging in the East ENSO: Recently turned neutral and trending toward El Niño this summer. This is a weak signal for April, so greater weight is placed on modeling and North American indices. Looking further down the road, El Niño later in the summer would mean a less active hurricane season in the Atlantic. Extended ECMWF, GFS, and CMC modeling: Overall pattern agreement is good, though differences exist. The ECMWF is drier than the GFS and Canadian. The GFS is slightly cooler than the ECMWF and Canadian. All show a Western U.S. trough and an East Coast ridge early in the month, evolving toward a trough over eastern Canada and the Northeast. The NOAA long-range forecast calls for slightly above-average precipitation and warmer-than-average temperatures. It was not a factor in this forecast. Mar 30, 2026 Monday Message for March 30, 2026 After a chilly last weekend in March, it's back to full-on springtime conditions for western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia. We discuss the Easter Week pattern shift on this week's Monday Message. Mar 30, 2026 See all Golf-O-Meter 0 Golfballs Only ducks or polar bears. Good day for knitting. Maybe, if you are brave. Worth taking a chance. A good day to be outside. Bosses, you'd better stand at the door. (Or will bosses be first out the door?) CONTACT US Privacy Policy Terms and Conditions Contact Us
